content.explanation:
➕ What Operators Do

Operators are symbols that perform an ACTION on values — combining them, comparing them, or doing math with them. You already met one operator last lesson without naming it: =, the assignment operator. Now we'll properly cover the main categories you'll use constantly: arithmetic (math), comparison, and logical operators.

🧮 Arithmetic Operators — Just Like Math Class

These work largely as you'd expect from school math, with one extra one you might not have seen: the remainder/modulus operator, which is genuinely useful in real programming (for things like checking if a number is even/odd, or cycling through a limited set of options).

⚖️ Comparison Operators — A Critical Distinction

This is one of the most important distinctions in this entire lesson, and a classic beginner trap: a single = ASSIGNS a value, while == or === COMPARES values. These look deceptively similar but do completely different jobs. Confusing them is one of the most common bugs beginners write, often without realizing why their code is behaving strangely.

🆚 == vs === — Why Three Equals Signs Exist

JavaScript actually has TWO different comparison operators, and the difference matters:

  • == (loose equality) — compares values but tries to CONVERT types to match if they're different, e.g. it considers 5 == "5" true, even though one is a number and one is text
  • === (strict equality) — compares both VALUE and TYPE, with no conversion. 5 === "5" is false, because one is a number and one is a string, even though they 'look' similar

Practical rule for this course: always use === (triple equals) unless you have a specific, deliberate reason not to. It avoids a whole category of confusing, hard-to-debug behavior that loose equality can cause.

🔗 Logical Operators — Combining Conditions

Sometimes you need to check MULTIPLE conditions at once. Logical operators let you combine comparisons: 'is the user logged in AND have they verified their email?' or 'is it Saturday OR Sunday?' You'll use these heavily once you reach conditionals in the next lesson.
content.syntax_breakdown:
🧱 Arithmetic Operators

console.log(5 + 3);  // 8   — addition
console.log(5 - 3);  // 2   — subtraction
console.log(5 * 3);  // 15  — multiplication
console.log(5 / 3);  // 1.666... — division
console.log(5 % 3);  // 2   — remainder (modulus)

The % (modulus) operator returns what's LEFT OVER after division. 5 % 3 means 'how many times does 3 fit into 5, and what's the remainder?' — 3 fits once, leaving 2. A very common real use: number % 2 === 0 checks if a number is even (no remainder when divided by 2).

🧱 Comparison Operators

console.log(5 === 5);   // true
console.log(5 === "5"); // false — different types
console.log(5 !== 3);   // true — "not equal to"
console.log(5 > 3);     // true
console.log(5 < 3);     // false
console.log(5 >= 5);    // true — greater than or equal to

Comparison operators always produce a Boolean result (true or false) — never anything else. This connects directly to what you learned about the Boolean data type last lesson.

🧱 Logical Operators

const isLoggedIn = true;
const hasVerifiedEmail = true;

console.log(isLoggedIn && hasVerifiedEmail); // true — BOTH must be true
console.log(isLoggedIn || hasVerifiedEmail); // true — AT LEAST ONE must be true
console.log(!isLoggedIn);                    // false — flips true to false (NOT)
  • && (AND) — true only if BOTH sides are true
  • || (OR) — true if AT LEAST ONE side is true
  • ! (NOT) — flips a Boolean to its opposite

🧪 String Concatenation — The + Operator's Other Job

const greeting = "Hello, " + "World";
console.log(greeting); // "Hello, World"

When used between two strings instead of two numbers, + joins them together — this is called 'concatenation.' Be careful: + behaves completely differently depending on whether you're using it on numbers or strings.
content.common_mistakes:
⚠️ Common Beginner Mistakes

  • Using = instead of === when comparing — a single = assigns a value (overwriting it!), it does NOT check equality.
  • Using == instead of === — loose equality can produce surprising, unintended matches between different types. Stick to === as a strong default habit.
  • Expecting + to add numbers when one side is actually a string — "5" + 3 produces "53" (concatenation), not 8 (addition), because one side is text.
  • Confusing && and || — && requires BOTH sides true; || only needs ONE side true.

mini_challenge.instruction:
🎯 Challenge:

1. In your script, create two number variables and use console.log() to print their sum using +
2. Use console.log() to compare two values using === 
3. Use console.log() to print the message: operators compare and calculate values
4. Below the script, add a paragraph (visible on the page) saying: operators compare and calculate values
5. Run and verify your code

quiz_engine.question: What does the === operator do differently from ==?
options: === checks value AND type with no conversion; == converts types to try to match
There is no real difference between them
=== is only used for numbers, == is only used for text
quiz_engine.explanation_feedback: Correct 🎉 === (strict equality) checks both value and type with no conversion. == (loose equality) tries to convert types to match, which can cause surprising, unintended results — stick with === as a default.

takeaways: Arithmetic operators: + - * / % (modulus returns the remainder after division)
A single = assigns a value; === compares value AND type
=== (strict equality) is safer and more predictable than == (loose equality)
&& (AND) requires both sides true; || (OR) needs at least one side true
+ on strings joins them together (concatenation), not numeric addition

cheat_sheet.===: Strict equality — checks value AND type (use this by default)
cheat_sheet.==: Loose equality — converts types, can cause unexpected results
cheat_sheet.&&: AND — true only if both sides are true
cheat_sheet.||: OR — true if at least one side is true
